content.text: Bash vs PowerShell in Azure Cloud Shell

Azure Cloud Shell provides two pre-authenticated, browser-based shell environments: Bash (running the Azure CLI) and PowerShell (running the Az module). Both connect to Azure automatically using your portal credentials, both have persistent storage, and both offer the same Azure API coverage. The choice is a matter of scripting style and tooling familiarity rather than capability.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison of Bash and PowerShell modes in Azure Cloud Shell

content.text: What Is Azure Cloud Shell?

Cloud Shell is a managed, ephemeral Linux container hosted by Microsoft. It starts in seconds from the Azure portal, the Azure mobile app, or shell.azure.com. The container is discarded after 20 minutes of inactivity, but your files survive in an Azure file share (5 GB, free) mounted at $HOME.
Pre-authenticated: your portal identity is automatically used—no az login or Connect-AzAccount needed.
Pre-installed tools: Azure CLI, Az PowerShell module, Terraform, kubectl, Helm, git, Python 3, Node.js, .NET, and more.
Persistent storage: $HOME is backed by an Azure Files share (clouddrive) that persists across sessions.
No installation required: runs entirely in the browser or via the mobile app.
Not for long-running jobs: the container terminates after 20 minutes of inactivity; use Azure Automation or a VM for scheduled automation.
Choosing Bash or PowerShell
Dimension
Bash (Azure CLI)
PowerShell (Az module)
Shell binary
bash 5.x
pwsh 7.x (PowerShell Core)
Primary Azure tool
az (Azure CLI)
Az module cmdlets
Output format
Text, JSON, TSV, YAML (az -o flag)
.NET objects (rich pipeline)
Text processing
grep, awk, sed, jq, cut
Where-Object, Select-Object, -match
Scripting files
.sh (bash scripts)
.ps1 (PowerShell scripts)
Object access
Requires --query or jq
Direct property access ($vm.Name)
Best fit
DevOps, Linux admins, pipeline scripting
Windows admins, .NET developers
Both available?
Yes — az works in the PS mode too
Yes — pwsh works in the Bash mode too
You can use az commands in PowerShell mode and run pwsh in Bash mode. The modes differ in which shell is the default and which tool is highlighted in IntelliSense, not in what is installed.
Bash Mode: Azure CLI
In Bash mode, az commands run natively. The shell behaves identically to a Linux terminal, so all standard Unix tools are available for processing CLI output.

# Cloud Shell Bash is already authenticated — no login needed
az account show --query "{name:name,sub:id}" -o table

# List all resource groups
az group list -o table

# Create a VM in 3 lines
az group create -n myRG -l eastus
az vm create -g myRG -n myVM --image Ubuntu2204LTS --size Standard_B1s   --admin-username azureuser --generate-ssh-keys

# Get the public IP
PUBLIC_IP=$(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM   --show-details --query publicIps -o tsv)
echo "VM public IP: $PUBLIC_IP"

# Combine az with standard Unix tools
# Count VMs per location
az vm list --query "[*].location" -o tsv | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# Find all storage accounts without HTTPS enforcement
az storage account list   --query "[?enableHttpsTrafficOnly==\`false\`].{name:name,rg:resourceGroup}"   -o table

# Watch a deployment in real time
az deployment group create -g myRG   --template-file ~/clouddrive/main.bicep   --no-wait
az deployment group list -g myRG --query "[0].properties.provisioningState"   -o tsv --watch 2>/dev/null || true
PowerShell Mode: Az Module
In PowerShell mode, Az cmdlets return .NET objects that compose with PowerShell's pipeline. The session is pre-authenticated; Get-AzContext shows your identity immediately.

# Cloud Shell PowerShell is already authenticated
Get-AzContext | Select-Object Account, Subscription, Tenant

# List resource groups
Get-AzResourceGroup | Format-Table ResourceGroupName, Location

# Create a VM
New-AzResourceGroup -Name myRG -Location eastus
New-AzVm -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myVM `
  -Location eastus -Image UbuntuLTS `
  -Size Standard_B1s -Credential (Get-Credential)

# Get the public IP as an object
$vm = Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName myRG -Name myVM
$nic = Get-AzNetworkInterface -ResourceId $vm.NetworkProfile.NetworkInterfaces[0].Id
$pip = Get-AzPublicIpAddress -ResourceGroupName myRG |
    Where-Object { $_.IpConfiguration.Id -eq $nic.IpConfigurations[0].Id }
$pip.IpAddress

# Object pipeline examples
# All VMs sorted by size, grouped by resource group
Get-AzVM | Sort-Object { $_.HardwareProfile.VmSize } |
    Group-Object ResourceGroupName |
    ForEach-Object {
        Write-Host "=== $($_.Name) ===" -ForegroundColor Cyan
        $_.Group | Select-Object Name, @{N="Size";E={$_.HardwareProfile.VmSize}}
    }

# Export VM inventory to CSV (persists to clouddrive)
Get-AzVM | Select-Object Name, ResourceGroupName, Location,
    @{N="Size";E={$_.HardwareProfile.VmSize}} |
    Export-Csv ~/clouddrive/vm-inventory.csv -NoTypeInformation
Write-Host "Saved to ~/clouddrive/vm-inventory.csv"
Switching Between Modes

You can switch the Cloud Shell environment mid-session from the toolbar dropdown (Bash ↔ PowerShell). The switch starts a new container but mounts the same $HOME file share, so your files are immediately available.

# In Bash mode: call pwsh directly to run a PowerShell one-liner
pwsh -Command "Get-AzVM | Select-Object Name, Location"

# Or start an interactive PowerShell sub-shell
pwsh

# In PowerShell mode: call bash or az directly
bash -c "az vm list -g myRG --query '[*].name' -o tsv"

# Or use the Azure CLI from within PowerShell (it returns JSON strings)
$result = az vm list -g myRG -o json | ConvertFrom-Json
$result | Select-Object name, location
When calling az from PowerShell mode, pipe to ConvertFrom-Json to get objects instead of strings. When calling pwsh from Bash mode, use -Command for a one-liner or enter interactively.
Persistent Storage: clouddrive

The ~/clouddrive directory is an Azure Files share automatically created in a storage account in your subscription. Everything saved here survives container termination and is accessible from the Azure portal's Storage Explorer.

# Check mounted clouddrive
df -h ~/clouddrive
# Typical output: //storagename.file.core.windows.net/fileshare  5.0G

# Upload a file to clouddrive from the portal toolbar (Upload button)
# Files land in ~/clouddrive/

# Download: right-click a file in the Cloud Shell file picker to download it

# Persist a Bash script for future sessions
cat > ~/clouddrive/deploy.sh << 'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
az group create -n ${RG:-myRG} -l ${LOCATION:-eastus}
EOF
chmod +x ~/clouddrive/deploy.sh

# Create a symlink for convenience
ln -sf ~/clouddrive/deploy.sh ~/deploy.sh

# Access clouddrive from PowerShell mode
Get-ChildItem ~/clouddrive

# Mount additional file shares (advanced)
clouddrive mount -s mySubId -g myRG -n mystorageacct -f myshare

# List shares currently mounted
clouddrive list
Pre-installed Tool Reference
Category
Tools Available in Both Modes
Azure
az (CLI), Az PowerShell module, bicep
Containers
kubectl, helm, docker (client only)
Infrastructure
terraform, ansible
Languages
python3, node.js, .NET 8, go, java
Source control
git
Network
curl, wget, ssh, ping, nslookup
Data
jq, yq, mysql client, psql
Editors
vim, nano, code (VS Code server via tunnel)

# Check versions of key tools
az version
terraform version
kubectl version --client
helm version --short
python3 --version
node --version

# Open VS Code in Cloud Shell (starts a VS Code Server tunnel)
code .
# Then open VS Code locally and connect to the forwarded tunnel
Working with Files and Persistent Storage
Cloud Shell mounts a 5 GB Azure file share at ~/clouddrive. Files there survive session restarts. The home directory (~) maps to ~/clouddrive/$USER so most tools write persistent files by default.

# Bash: check and use clouddrive
df -h ~/clouddrive            # shows the Azure file share mount
ls ~/clouddrive               # persistent files

# Upload a file via the Cloud Shell toolbar Upload/Download button
# then reference it:
cat ~/myconfig.json

# Download a file to your local machine
download ~/clouddrive/output.csv

# Create subdirectories for project files
mkdir -p ~/clouddrive/scripts/azure
cp deploy.sh ~/clouddrive/scripts/azure/

# PowerShell: same clouddrive, object-oriented approach
Get-PSDrive -Name CloudDrive   # shows the Azure file share

# List all files in clouddrive
Get-ChildItem ~/clouddrive -Recurse

# Copy files to clouddrive for persistence
Copy-Item ./deploy.ps1 ~/clouddrive/scripts/

# Read a config file
$config = Get-Content ~/clouddrive/config.json | ConvertFrom-Json
Write-Host "Environment: $($config.environment)"
Files outside ~/clouddrive are ephemeral: they exist only for the current session. The /tmp directory, installed packages, and compiled binaries are lost when the session ends.
Tip: Store scripts and configuration in ~/clouddrive. For packages (pip, npm, go modules), re-install at session start or add install commands to ~/.bashrc or $PROFILE.
Cloud Shell Limitations and Gotchas
Cloud Shell is a managed environment with constraints you must plan for.
Constraint
Detail
Workaround
20-minute idle timeout
Session terminates after 20 min with no input
Use --no-wait for long ops; poll with az deployment show
No persistent background processes
Daemons and long-running jobs are killed on timeout
Use Azure Container Instances or VMs instead
No root/sudo access
Package manager installs require --user flag
Use pip install --user or rely on pre-installed tools
Single region
Cloud Shell runs in the region selected at setup
Consider local install for latency-sensitive operations
5 GB storage cap
clouddrive file share has a 5 GB limit
Archive old files or use Azure Blob Storage
No GPU or heavy compute
CPU and RAM are limited
Use Azure VMs, Batch, or ML for heavy compute

# Check Cloud Shell region and storage
az account show --query environmentName -o tsv
df -h ~/clouddrive

# Install a Python package without root
pip install --user requests pandas
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"   # add to ~/.bashrc

# Use a pre-installed language runtime (avoid installing new runtimes)
python3 --version    # 3.x pre-installed
node --version       # LTS pre-installed
dotnet --version     # .NET SDK pre-installed
go version           # Go pre-installed

# Use --no-wait for long-running deployments
az deployment group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --template-file main.bicep \
  --no-wait

# Poll for completion
watch -n 10 'az deployment group show \
  -g myRG -n main \
  --query properties.provisioningState -o tsv'
VS Code Integration with Cloud Shell
Cloud Shell includes a built-in Monaco editor and supports VS Code Server tunnels, letting you edit files with a full IDE experience without leaving the browser.

# Open the built-in Monaco editor
# Click the '{...}' (Open editor) icon in the Cloud Shell toolbar
# The editor appears on the right; file tree is on the left

# Start a VS Code tunnel (full VS Code in browser)
code tunnel
# Prints a device code and URL. Open the URL, sign in with Microsoft account,
# and full VS Code opens connected to your Cloud Shell.

# The tunnel requires authentication:
# Visit https://github.com/login/device and enter the displayed code

# Files are in ~/clouddrive - edit, save, and run from the integrated terminal

# Same VS Code tunnel works from PowerShell mode
code tunnel

# Or use the built-in editor via the toolbar icon
# Monaco editor shows ~/clouddrive files

# Run the currently open script from the integrated terminal:
. ./deploy.ps1

# Navigate files with the tree on the left side of the editor
# All edits auto-save to ~/clouddrive
The VS Code tunnel session counts against the 20-minute timeout — activity in the editor resets it. The tunnel requires a Microsoft account login separate from your Azure account.
Equivalent Workflows: Bash vs PowerShell
The same Azure management tasks are achievable in both shells. Here are common workflows side by side.
Listing and Filtering Resources

# Bash: list VMs in a resource group, filter by status
az vm list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --show-details \
  --query "[?powerState=='VM running'].{Name:name,Size:hardwareProfile.vmSize}" \
  -o table

# Count resources by type
az resource list --resource-group myRG -o json | \
  jq 'group_by(.type) | map({type: .[0].type, count: length})' | \
  jq -r '.[] | "\(.count)\t\(.type)"' | sort -rn

# PowerShell: list VMs in a resource group, filter by status
Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName myRG -Status |
  Where-Object { $_.PowerState -eq 'VM running' } |
  Select-Object Name, @{N='Size';E={$_.HardwareProfile.VmSize}}

# Count resources by type
Get-AzResource -ResourceGroupName myRG |
  Group-Object ResourceType |
  Select-Object Count, Name |
  Sort-Object Count -Descending
Creating and Tagging Resources

# Bash: create a storage account with tags
az storage account create \
  --name mystore$RANDOM \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard_LRS \
  --tags env=prod owner=team@example.com

# Update tags on existing resources
az tag update \
  --resource-id $(az resource show -g myRG -n myVM --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines --query id -o tsv) \
  --operation merge \
  --tags 'cost-center=engineering' 'project=alpha'

# PowerShell: create a storage account with tags
$suffix = -join ((48..57) | Get-Random -Count 6 | ForEach-Object { [char]$_ })
New-AzStorageAccount `
  -ResourceGroupName myRG `
  -Name "mystore$suffix" `
  -Location eastus `
  -SkuName Standard_LRS `
  -Tag @{ env='prod'; owner='team@example.com' }

# Update tags on an existing resource
$resource = Get-AzResource -ResourceGroupName myRG -ResourceName myVM
Update-AzTag -ResourceId $resource.Id -Tag @{'cost-center'='engineering'; project='alpha'} -Operation Merge
Automating Cloud Shell Tasks from CI/CD Pipelines

Cloud Shell requires interactive browser authentication and cannot be called directly from CI/CD pipelines. Use the Azure CLI or Az PowerShell module with a service principal or managed identity instead.

# GitHub Actions: equivalent of Cloud Shell Bash
name: Deploy via Azure CLI
on: [push]
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          creds: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CREDENTIALS }}
      - name: Run Azure CLI script
        run: |
          az group create --name myRG --location eastus
          az deployment group create \
            --resource-group myRG \
            --template-file ./main.bicep \
            --parameters ./params.json

# GitHub Actions: equivalent of Cloud Shell PowerShell
name: Deploy via Az PowerShell
on: [push]
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          creds: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CREDENTIALS }}
          enable-AzPSSession: true
      - name: Run Az PowerShell script
        uses: azure/powershell@v2
        with:
          azPSVersion: "latest"
          inlineScript: |
            $rg = Get-AzResourceGroup -Name "myRG" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
            if (-not $rg) {
              New-AzResourceGroup -Name "myRG" -Location "eastus"
            }
            New-AzResourceGroupDeployment `
              -ResourceGroupName "myRG" `
              -TemplateFile "./main.bicep" `
              -TemplateParameterFile "./params.json"
Cloud Shell is for interactive exploration and ad-hoc management. For repeatable, automated deployments, use CI/CD pipelines with service principals or managed identities.
Session Lifecycle and Best Practices
Understanding the Cloud Shell session lifecycle helps you avoid losing work and design efficient interactive workflows.
Every Cloud Shell session starts fresh except for ~/clouddrive. Re-install ephemeral tools or add install commands to ~/.bashrc (Bash) or $PROFILE (PowerShell).
The 20-minute idle timer resets on any keypress or command output. Long-running commands keep the session alive.
If your session times out mid-command, the command is killed. For critical operations use --no-wait and poll for completion.
Store all scripts in ~/clouddrive before ending a session. The Bash $HISTFILE is persistent; PowerShell history is not persisted by default.
Use az configure --defaults group=myRG location=eastus to set CLI defaults. PowerShell: use Set-AzDefault -ResourceGroupName 'myRG'.
Avoid opening multiple Cloud Shell sessions simultaneously — each mounts the same clouddrive file share, which can cause file conflicts.

# Add persistent setup to ~/.bashrc (Bash)
cat >> ~/.bashrc << 'BASHEOF'
# Azure defaults
export AZURE_DEFAULTS_GROUP="myRG"
export AZURE_DEFAULTS_LOCATION="eastus"

# PATH additions
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"

# Aliases
alias azls='az resource list -o table'
alias deploys='az deployment group list -g $AZURE_DEFAULTS_GROUP -o table'
BASHEOF

source ~/.bashrc

# Add persistent setup to $PROFILE (PowerShell)
# $PROFILE is ~/clouddrive/.config/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1
if (!(Test-Path $PROFILE)) {
    New-Item -ItemType File -Path $PROFILE -Force
}

Add-Content $PROFILE @'
# Set Az defaults
Set-AzDefault -ResourceGroupName "myRG"

# Useful aliases
Set-Alias -Name azls -Value Get-AzResource
function Get-Deployments { Get-AzDeployment -ResourceGroupName "myRG" | Select-Object DeploymentName, ProvisioningState }
'@

. $PROFILE   # reload the profile
Summary

Azure Cloud Shell provides two pre-authenticated shell environments: Bash (using the Azure CLI) and PowerShell (using the Az module). Bash suits Unix-fluent workflows with text pipelines (grep, awk, jq), while PowerShell suits .NET/Windows admins who prefer object pipelines (Where-Object, Select-Object). Both modes share the same persistent ~/clouddrive file share, the same pre-installed tool set, and the same Azure API coverage. You can switch modes from the toolbar or call one shell from within the other. Cloud Shell is ideal for interactive management and short scripts; use Azure Automation or a VM for unattended long-running tasks.
